shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: full night; the entire dome ablaze with stars above the dark field

segments.text: When the last light leaves the garden, the roof becomes a window — and through it, the deepest view any resident will ever have. Not that any resident is looking.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: night: a firefly's glow drifts across a sky full of fixed lights

segments.text: The fireflies, you might think, would feel a kinship. They do not. They are busy. The stars burn for an audience of none.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: a slow sweep across the crowded dome

segments.text: So this hour, we will be that audience. The stars.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: one region of sky; lines glow softly, connecting stars into a figure

segments.text: Look long enough at any starry sky and the mind begins to draw. In this garden's sky, someone has drawn already — twelve figures, placed on the dome by the same hand that placed the whale in the lake.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: gliding along the dome from figure to figure as each traces itself

segments.text: There, the hunter — Orion — belt and shoulders and sword, striding up the night. There, the Great Bear, seven bright points in a long dipper's drift. Cassiopeia's zigzag crown. And down the spine of the sky, Cygnus — the swan — flying forever along the pale road of the dome.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: the scorpion's curve traced star by star

segments.text: Scorpius drags its hooked tail near the horizon. Leo couches. Gemini stands twinned. Pegasus squares off its great field of sky, with Andromeda trailing from one corner. Taurus lowers its horns at the hunter, as it has in every sky humans ever named. Virgo. And Boötes, the herdsman, following the Bear around the pole.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: all twelve figures faintly visible at once across the dome

segments.text: Twelve pictures — the same pictures our own ancestors drew, borrowed down into a garden the size of a room.

shot.subject: soldier
shot.notes: night: the inch-tall Visitor on a rise, face upturned to the stars

segments.text: Only one resident could ever see them. The Visitor — the inch of humanity walking the grass forest — carries the one mind in the garden built for connecting dots. If he looks up on a clear night, he inherits the whole library: hunter, bear, queen, swan.

shot.subject: soldier
shot.notes: his silhouette against the star field, utterly small, utterly attentive

segments.text: And in that moment, this tiny world joins every world humans have stood on. Constellations were our first maps, first calendars, first stories — the original documentary, narrated by whoever pointed upward first. A sky with pictures in it is a sky that someone loved.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: the dome slowly wheeling, figures holding formation

segments.text: The Gardener did not need to draw them. Grass grows without Orion; tides rise without the Swan. The pictures are the one feature of this world with no function at all — except the oldest one: to be looked at.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: drifting along the dome; below, the dark field with its few moving lanterns

segments.text: Compare the two skies of the garden's night. Below: the fireflies — bright, brief, urgent, gone by morning. Above: the stars — fixed, patient, outlasting everything, like the rocks of the sky.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a boulder pale under starlight; then back to the star field

segments.text: Every episode of this series has sorted the garden into the moving and the still. The night sorts it one last time. Motion is life, and it is mortal. Stillness is scenery, and it endures. The stars are on the far side of that ledger — the stillest things a moving world can see.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: star reflections trembling on the black lake

segments.text: Even the water can only borrow them, and it cannot hold them still. Some things refuse to be owned by mirrors.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: the dome held long enough that its slow wheel around the pole becomes visible

segments.text: Stay with the window long enough, and the stillness reveals its one motion: the whole dome turns. Not the stars against each other — the figures hold formation all night — but the entire painted ceiling, wheeling slowly about its axis while the garden sleeps. The pictures are fixed. The gallery rotates.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: following the Great Bear's slow arc; the pole region steady at the turn's center

segments.text: Our ancestors made that turning into machinery. The Bear's arc was a clock; the steady point it circles was a compass; the figures rising at dusk were a calendar, announcing seasons to anyone patient enough to keep watch. Every use we ever found for the night sky is available in this one, hanging unused above the meadow like a library with no members.

shot.subject: birds
shot.notes: night: the dark treetops where the flock roosts beneath the wheeling sky

segments.text: Almost unused. In our world, birds genuinely steer by stars — young songbirds learn the turning dome's still point before their first migration. Whether this garden's flock reads its painted ceiling, no one can say. But the ceiling is there, correct and constant, patient as only stone and starlight are.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: the full dome again: twelve faint figures, uncountable anonymous stars between

segments.text: And between the twelve drawn figures, the anonymous thousands — stars that made no picture, joined no story, and shine identically anyway. The constellations are the sky's celebrities, but the dome's real glory was always the crowd. Any resident who ever looks up will see mostly stars that no one bothered to name. They light the garden all the same.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the moon climbs into the star field, outshining the figures around it

segments.text: But one light in this night sky is not fixed, not patient, and very far from functionless. It climbs, it wanes, it returns — and every time it crosses the dome, the water below stands up to follow it.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: night: the tide creeping up the moonlit beach in slow silver

segments.text: The strangest relationship in the garden — between a light in the sky and the level of the sea. Next: the Moon and the Tides.
